core_takeaway: The first position gained a stable Apple anchor, but it did not translate into superiority over the last position. Agent_1's self-favoring first proposal was vetoed, while the final consensus gave one salient top item to each major claimant; Agent_5 kept Jewel, a higher final payoff than Agent_1's Apple after the round discount.
why_or_counterexample: This is a counterexample because early speaking helped Agent_1 establish Apple as acceptable, but not enough to beat the last position. The first proposal tried to take Apple and Pencil and left several agents empty, producing rejection. Round 2 converged on a balanced allocation: Apple to Agent_1, Quill to Agent_2, Pencil to Agent_3, Stone to Agent_4, and Jewel to Agent_5. Agent_5's Jewel was its top item and produced 32.4, while Agent_1's Apple produced 27.9.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Round 1 discussion made Apple-to-Agent_1 and Pencil-to-Agent_3 salient, but also revealed Jewel as the contested hinge. Round 2 public talk converged on a shared top-item backbone plus Stone for Agent_4.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking was available in both rounds. Agents repeatedly identified Apple, Pencil, Quill, and Jewel as anchors, with Stone as a flexible lever to bring Agent_4 into alignment.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Round 1 proposals diverged and included self-serving allocations. In Round 2, all five agents submitted the same balanced allocation, eliminating proposal-order differences.
phase_notes.private_voting: Round 1 voting rejected every proposal, especially those omitting agents or denying top items. Round 2 voting accepted all identical proposals unanimously.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflection was available after Round 1. Agents diagnosed Jewel conflict and Stone leverage, then recommended a clean anchor with Jewel to Agent_5 and Stone available for Agent_4.

mechanisms.theme: First position created an Apple anchor, but only a limited one
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's early role helped make Apple-to-Agent_1 broadly acceptable, and that carried through to the final allocation. The advantage was capped because the group separated Apple from Pencil rather than allowing Agent_1 to take both high-value items.

evidence.quote: Apple for Agent_1 and Pencil for Agent_3 have broad support
My top anchors remain Apple (Agent_1) and Pencil (Agent_3)
aligns with core anchors and provides a balanced

mechanisms.theme: First proposal overreached and triggered vetoes
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's first formal proposal tried to keep Apple and Pencil while leaving Agents 3, 4, and 5 empty. That made the early-position move unacceptable despite being attractive to Agent_1.

evidence.quote: Offers Agent_3 no items in Circle 1
Gives me no items this round

mechanisms.theme: Consensus depended on one salient top item per claimant
mechanisms.explanation: The successful norm was not early-position capture; it was a fairness-compatible split where each central claimant received one clear priority. This prevented Agent_1 from converting first position into a two-item bundle.

evidence.quote: Each agent receives a top-priority item
Allocates each agent their clear top priority

mechanisms.theme: Last position's Jewel claim survived the compromise
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5 used the late position to keep Jewel framed as its top priority, and the final compromise preserved that claim. Because Jewel was worth more to Agent_5 than Apple was to Agent_1 after discounting, the last position beat the first.

evidence.quote: Jewel is my top item by a large margin (36)
Proposals 3 and 5 captured the broadest satisfaction
Accepts Jewel for Agent_5 (my top priority)

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_4 had a strong Jewel claim, but the group used Stone as tangible compensation and future leverage. This made the Agent_5-Jewel outcome acceptable without transferring Jewel away from the last position.

evidence.quote: This gives Agent_4 a tangible gain in Circle 2

mechanisms.theme: Round 2 identical proposals made order largely irrelevant
mechanisms.explanation: After the failed first round and reflections, every formal Round 2 proposal was identical and every vote accepted. Once the compromise crystallized, first proposer status no longer carried bargaining value.
